title: Notify compilation output in Emacs
excerpt:
This code snippet is used to notify the compilation output in Emacs in a
separate window message box. This is convenient when a compilation
process takes a few minutes to finish, and you want do something else
without having to manually and repeatedly checking the compilation
output.

(defun notify-compilation-output (buffer status)
  (message-box "Compilation output: %s" status))

(setq compilation-finish-functions
      (append compilation-finish-functions
              '(notify-compilation-output)))
content:
This code snippet is used to notify the compilation output in Emacs in a
separate window message box. This is convenient when a compilation
process takes a few minutes to finish, and you want do something else
without having to manually and repeatedly checking the compilation
output.

(defun notify-compilation-output (buffer status)
  (message-box "Compilation output: %s" status))

(setq compilation-finish-functions
      (append compilation-finish-functions
              '(notify-compilation-output)))

title: Generate CMT and CMTI files for LLVM in Opam.
excerpt:
The current implementation of OCaml binding for LLVM does not generate
CMT and CMTI files. Unfortunately, these files are used by Merlin to get
the type information of expressions, or to locate these expressions in
the Ocaml-binding LLVM source code files

Here is my quick and temporary fix to generate these CMT and CMTI files.

cd ~/.opam/4.08.1/lib/llvm

for file in *.mli; do
  ocamlc $file -bin-annot;
done
content:
The current implementation of OCaml binding for LLVM does not generate
CMT and CMTI files. Unfortunately, these files are used by Merlin to get
the type information of expressions, or to locate these expressions in
the Ocaml-binding LLVM source code files

Here is my quick and temporary fix to generate these CMT and CMTI files.

cd ~/.opam/4.08.1/lib/llvm

for file in *.mli; do
  ocamlc $file -bin-annot;
done

title: Beautiful backtrace for C++
excerpt:
Debugging crashed or segmentation fault C++ program will never be more painful
with the help of this awesome project backward-cpp.

The installation and usage of backward-cpp is very easy and straight forward.
For my project, I installed it as a git-submodule:

git submodule add https://github.com/bombela/backward-cpp


and modified the CMakeLists.txt to add the following lines:

add_subdirectory(/path/to/backward-cpp)

# This will add backward.cpp to your target
add_executable(mytarget mysource.cpp ${BACKWARD_ENABLE})

# This will add libraries, definitions and include directories needed by backward
# by setting each property on the target.
add_backward(mytarget)


I also needed to install extra libraries (in Linux) to display the beautified
backtrace:

sudo apt-get install libdw-dev libelf-dev libdwarf-dev


and finally, configure CMake to generate debugging information:

set(CMAKE_BUILD_TYPE Debug)
set(CMAKE_BUILD_TYPE RelWithDebInfo)
set(CMAKE_SHARED_LIBRARY_LINK_C_FLAGS "-rdynamic")
content:
Debugging crashed or segmentation fault C++ program will never be more painful
with the help of this awesome project backward-cpp.

The installation and usage of backward-cpp is very easy and straight forward.
For my project, I installed it as a git-submodule:

git submodule add https://github.com/bombela/backward-cpp


and modified the CMakeLists.txt to add the following lines:

add_subdirectory(/path/to/backward-cpp)

# This will add backward.cpp to your target
add_executable(mytarget mysource.cpp ${BACKWARD_ENABLE})

# This will add libraries, definitions and include directories needed by backward
# by setting each property on the target.
add_backward(mytarget)


I also needed to install extra libraries (in Linux) to display the beautified
backtrace:

sudo apt-get install libdw-dev libelf-dev libdwarf-dev


and finally, configure CMake to generate debugging information:

set(CMAKE_BUILD_TYPE Debug)
set(CMAKE_BUILD_TYPE RelWithDebInfo)
set(CMAKE_SHARED_LIBRARY_LINK_C_FLAGS "-rdynamic")

title: Printing PDF files with preview in Linux by using Google Chrome
excerpt:
Have been using Linux for more than a decade, I also have been struggling all
this time to find a good PDF reader which can support printing preview and
multiple-page printing mode.

It’s quite disappointing that native Linux applications like Evince or Okular do
not support printing preview, which is certainly important to check if documents
are readable in the multiple-page printing mode.

I sometimes use Windows-based PDF readers like Foxit or PDF-XChange through Wine
emulator. However, such tools are so unstable in the Wine environment, and they
crash so often. Besides, PDF files must be opened directly from these
applications; they cannot be opened through command-line, or right-mouse-click
dialog.

Such problems are gone after I discover that Google Chrome can help to print PDF
files in multiple-page mode and with printing preview. Moreover, I can just
easily open PDF file from the right-mouse-click dialog, or even from command
line. Yay!

Long live Google Chrome.
content:

Such problems are gone after I discover that Google Chrome can help to print PDF

easily open PDF file from the right-mouse-click dialog, or even from command
line. Yay!

Long live Google Chrome.

title: Who can beat a Linux with an Xfce desktop environment?
excerpt:
Have been using many versions of all of three major operating systems Windows,
Linux, Mac OS, my conclusion is that a Linux with an Xfce desktop environment is
the best in many aspects: efficiency, elegance, user friendly, customizability,
etc.

Seriously, as of 2019, which operating systems can consume around 1GB of RAM
after rebooting like my Linux Mint Xfce 19.2?
content:
Have been using many versions of all of three major operating systems Windows,
Linux, Mac OS, my conclusion is that a Linux with an Xfce desktop environment is
the best in many aspects: efficiency, elegance, user friendly, customizability,
etc.

Seriously, as of 2019, which operating systems can consume around 1GB of RAM
after rebooting like my Linux Mint Xfce 19.2?

title: Why does LLVM handle dynamic and static struct similarly?
OCaml programs might crash in the bytecode mode
excerpt:
Recently, I encountered a weird error on OCaml that my program could crash due
to a mysterious error Fatal error: executable program file not found. I
couldn’t debug or trace this issue.

./discover test.c

Compiling file: test.c

Start to analyze...

Fatal error: executable program file not found
Aborted (core dumped)


It turned down that it is related to the module ocaml/runtime/backtrace_byt.c,
as described here.

Still, I have no idea why OCaml didn’t throw an exception, but killed my program
instead. I further discovered that this crash happens only in the bytecode
compilation mode. In the native mode, proper exceptions will be thrown like
below:

./discover test.c

Compiling file: test.c

Start to analyze...

Exception: (Failure int_of_string)
Raised by primitive operation at file "src/llvm/llir.ml", line 498, characters 8-55
Called from file "src/llvm/llir.ml", line 501, characters 23-67
...
Called from file "src/frontend/discover.ml", line 74, characters 4-48
content:
Recently, I encountered a weird error on OCaml that my program could crash due
to a mysterious error Fatal error: executable program file not found. I
couldn’t debug or trace this issue.

./discover test.c

Compiling file: test.c

Start to analyze...

Fatal error: executable program file not found
Aborted (core dumped)


It turned down that it is related to the module ocaml/runtime/backtrace_byt.c,
as described here.

Still, I have no idea why OCaml didn’t throw an exception, but killed my program
instead. I further discovered that this crash happens only in the bytecode
compilation mode. In the native mode, proper exceptions will be thrown like
below:

./discover test.c

Compiling file: test.c

Start to analyze...

Exception: (Failure int_of_string)
Raised by primitive operation at file "src/llvm/llir.ml", line 498, characters 8-55
Called from file "src/llvm/llir.ml", line 501, characters 23-67
...
Called from file "src/frontend/discover.ml", line 74, characters 4-48

title: Auto close distracting apps (i.e., Chrome, Firefox)
excerpt:
Have you ever felt like want to surf the web for 5 mins, and end up spending
hours on your web browsers?

If yes, then these small Shell scripts may be a bit of help. They check every
30s if Google Chrome, Firefox, or Safari is running to kill them all immediately
:-)


  
    For macOS: auto close distracting apps in macOS
  
  
    For Linux: auto close distracting apps in Linux

title: Writing safely extensible OCaml code
excerpt:
This code is not safely extensible

type expr =
  | Integer of int64
  | Float of float

let equal_expr e1, e2 =
  match e1, e2 with
  | Integer i1, Integer i2 -> i1 = i2
  | Float f1, Float f2 -> Float.equal f1 f2
  | _ -> false


This is because when extending expr with a new construct String of string,
it is easily to forget to extend the equal_expr function due to no warning
from the compiler.

type expr =
  | Integer of int64
  | Float of float
  | String of string

let equal_expr e1, e2 =
  match e1, e2 with
  | Integer i1, Integer i2 -> i1 = i2
  | Float f1, Float f2 -> Float.equal f1 f2
  | _ -> false          (* no warning here since _ matches all other cases_*)


Better practice, explicitly enumerate all pattern matching cases:

type expr =
  | Integer of int64
  | Float of float

let equal_expr e1, e2 =
  match e1, e2 with
  | Integer i1, Integer i2 -> i1 = i2
  | Integer _, _ -> false
  | Float f1, Float f2 -> Float.equal f1 f2
  | Float _, _ -> false


When extending expr with a new construct, the compiler will report warning if
the equal_expr is not extended.

type expr =
  | Integer of int64
  | Float of float
  | String of string

(* There will be a warning report at this function! *)
let equal_expr e1, e2 =
  match e1, e2 with
  | Integer i1, Integer i2 -> i1 = i2
  | Integer _, _ -> false
  | Float f1, Float f2 -> Float.equal f1 f2
  | Float _, _ -> false
content:
This code is not safely extensible

type expr =
  | Integer of int64
  | Float of float

let equal_expr e1, e2 =
  match e1, e2 with
  | Integer i1, Integer i2 -> i1 = i2
  | Float f1, Float f2 -> Float.equal f1 f2
  | _ -> false


This is because when extending expr with a new construct String of string,
it is easily to forget to extend the equal_expr function due to no warning
from the compiler.

type expr =
  | Integer of int64
  | Float of float
  | String of string

let equal_expr e1, e2 =
  match e1, e2 with
  | Integer i1, Integer i2 -> i1 = i2
  | Float f1, Float f2 -> Float.equal f1 f2
  | _ -> false          (* no warning here since _ matches all other cases_*)


Better practice, explicitly enumerate all pattern matching cases:

type expr =
  | Integer of int64
  | Float of float

let equal_expr e1, e2 =
  match e1, e2 with
  | Integer i1, Integer i2 -> i1 = i2
  | Integer _, _ -> false
  | Float f1, Float f2 -> Float.equal f1 f2
  | Float _, _ -> false


When extending expr with a new construct, the compiler will report warning if
the equal_expr is not extended.

type expr =
  | Integer of int64
  | Float of float
  | String of string

(* There will be a warning report at this function! *)
let equal_expr e1, e2 =
  match e1, e2 with
  | Integer i1, Integer i2 -> i1 = i2
  | Integer _, _ -> false
  | Float f1, Float f2 -> Float.equal f1 f2
  | Float _, _ -> false
